statement.markdown:
You are given an array of non‑negative integers where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. Starting at index 0, determine whether it is possible to reach the last index of the array.

Formally, for an array A of length n, you start at position 0. From position i you may move to any position j such that i < j ≤ i + A[i]. Your task is to decide if there exists a sequence of jumps that lands exactly on index n‑1.

If the last index is reachable print "YES", otherwise print "NO".

The solution must run in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer n, the length of the array.
The second line contains n space‑separated integers A[0], A[1], ..., A[n‑1] representing the maximum jump length from each position.
statement.outputFormat: Print a single line containing either "YES" if the last index can be reached, or "NO" otherwise.
statement.notes: The array may contain zeros. A zero at a position blocks further progress unless that position can be bypassed by a previous jump.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array.
Maximum jump length from index i.

examples.explanation: From index 0 jump 2 steps to index 2, then jump 2 steps to index 4 (the last index).
You get stuck at index 3 because A[3] = 0 and cannot reach the last index.

hints.text: Try to solve the problem first by thinking about the constraints and the problem statement.
You can start at index 0 and try to reach the last index by checking if the current index plus the maximum jump length is greater than or equal to the last index.
To solve this problem in O(n) time, you can use a dynamic programming approach where you keep track of the maximum reachable index at each step.
The key to solving this problem efficiently is to realize that you only need to keep track of the maximum reachable index, not the actual path taken to reach that index.
You can use a single variable to keep track of the maximum reachable index, and then use a loop to iterate over the array and update the maximum reachable index at each step.

editorial.title: Jump Game I: From Exhaustive Search to Greedy Reach
editorial.intuition: The problem asks whether we can reach the last index of an array by jumping forward up to a given number of steps from each position. A naive approach would try every possible path, but the array can have up to 100 000 elements, making that infeasible. The key observation is that we only need to know the furthest index we can reach so far; if the current index is beyond that, we are stuck. This leads to a simple greedy scan that updates the maximum reachable position as we iterate.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute‑force solution would treat the array as a graph where each index has directed edges to the next up to A[i] indices. One could run a depth‑first or breadth‑first search, marking visited nodes and exploring all outgoing edges. Although correct, this approach visits each edge once, resulting in O(∑A[i]) time, which can be as large as O(n²) in the worst case. With n=100 000, that is far too slow and would exceed time limits.
editorial.betterSolution: The greedy method keeps a single variable, maxReach, representing the furthest index reachable from the start using any sequence of jumps processed so far. While iterating over the array, if the current index i exceeds maxReach, we cannot proceed and the answer is NO. Otherwise we update maxReach to max(maxReach, i + A[i]). If at any point maxReach reaches or surpasses the last index, we can immediately answer YES. This runs in linear time and constant space.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is the greedy scan described above. It processes each element once, updates a single integer, and terminates as soon as the goal is reachable. This matches the known time complexity O(n) and space complexity O(1).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Assume we have processed indices 0…i−1 and maxReach is the furthest index reachable from the start using only those indices. If i>maxReach, no sequence of jumps can reach i, so the last index is unreachable. If i≤maxReach, we can reach i, and from i we can jump to any index up to i+A[i]. Thus the new furthest reachable index is max(maxReach, i+A[i]). By induction, after processing all indices, maxReach equals the true maximum reachable index. Therefore, if maxReach≥n−1 we can reach the end, otherwise we cannot.
editorial.dryRun: Consider arr=[2,3,1,1,4]. Start: maxReach=0. i=0, jump=2 → maxReach=2. i=1≤2, jump=3 → maxReach=4. i=2≤4, jump=1 → maxReach stays 4. i=3≤4, jump=1 → maxReach stays 4. At i=4 we have reached the last index, so answer YES. The algorithm never backtracks and uses only a single integer.
commonMistakes: Assuming that a smaller jump earlier can always be compensated by a larger jump later; the greedy update must consider all jumps seen so far.
Checking reachability only at the end instead of early; this misses the opportunity to terminate when the last index is already reachable.
Using a visited array or recursion, which wastes O(n) space and may hit recursion limits.
Treating the array as a graph and performing BFS/DFS, leading to O(n²) time in worst case.
Forgetting to handle the case where n=1, which should immediately return YES.
Misinterpreting A[i] as a fixed jump length rather than a maximum; the algorithm must allow any jump length up to A[i].
